title: How I Built an App in 2 Days? (Dos and Don’ts of Vibecoding)
author: Saumya Nishi
text:
How I Built an App in 2 Days? (Dos and Don’ts of Vibecoding)
From fuzzy idea to working prototype in a 48-hour sprint
The Problem That Started It All
Chats sprawl. Files disappear. The few answers worth keeping get buried in endless conversations. I’d experienced this frustration dozens of times, but this time I paused: was this just my problem, or something others face too?
Instead of diving straight into code, I spent 30 minutes validating the concept. I asked five colleagues a simple question: “How do you currently save useful information from AI chats?” The responses were telling — sticky notes, random documents, screenshots they’d never find again. Everyone had the same messy workaround.
That validation gave me confidence to commit 48 hours to building a focused solution: a quiet notetaker with a companion notebook where you ask questions against curated files, then clip the best responses with proper source tracking.
The Framework: Validate Before You Build
This sprint followed the Idea-to-App Vibe Framework (IAVF) — a structured approach that prevents the common trap of building solutions nobody wants. Here’s how it played out:
Phase 1: Clarity (2 hours)
- Validated the problem existed for others
- Defined success: “Can I find and reuse a saved insight in under a minute?”
- Identified the core user flow: Ask → Answer → Save → Reuse
- Set constraints: Two-panel interface, works with PDFs, no complexity
Phase 2: AI Collaboration (Day 1)
Started with interface design, not backend logic. I treated the AI like a design partner, using structured prompts that maintained consistent vision throughout the build.
Phase 3: Continuous Testing (Throughout)
After every major change, I ran the same simple test: ask a question, save the response, find it later. This caught usability issues before they became problems.
Day 1: Shape the Surface
I began with a conversation, not code:
My opening prompt: “Be my product partner. We’re making a simple notetaker with companion notebook. The user uploads a few PDFs, asks questions, gets answers with source hints, and saves the best responses. Success means finding saved insights quickly. Propose a calm, two-panel layout.”
The AI suggested a clean split: one tab for Q&A, and one for saved items. This foundation shaped every subsequent decision.
Key moves that maintained momentum:
- Guardrails first: “Don’t change colors or layout without asking”
- Plain English requests: “Show an error message if the file is too big” not “Implement file size validation with client-side exception handling”
- One change per prompt: Added search, then tested, then moved on
Day 2: Behavior and Polish
Day 2 focused on the details that separate demos from tools people actually use:
- Clear error states: “Not found in your files” instead of confusing technical messages
- Helpful suggestions: When searches failed, the app suggested related questions
- Micro-interactions: Subtle fades when saving items, not distracting animations
- Mobile responsiveness: Tested on phone throughout, not as an afterthought
The validation moment: I searched an old brief I’d rewritten three times. The answer appeared with source context. One click saved it. Two days later, I reused that snippet for a new proposal in under a minute.
Your Prompt Library
These prompts maintained consistency while building iteratively:
- Set Guardrails — Be my product partner for a [description of app]. Confirm the purpose, main user, and success criteria. List assumptions you’ll keep unless I change them. Don’t modify audience, colors, or layout without asking.
- Design the Interface — Propose a [layout description] that feels [mood/tone]. Choose one gentle main color and supporting colors that are easy on the eyes. Write friendly interface copy. Explain each section’s purpose.
- Add Core Behavior — Set up this user flow: [step-by-step path]. When errors occur, show friendly messages in plain English. If processing takes time, show “Thinking…” with cancel option.
- Test and Refine — Give me 5 realistic test scenarios. Tell me what good responses look like. Create a 2-minute demo check: one normal use, one error case, one success moment.
- Polish for Real Use — Add subtle motion: gentle fades only. Make text readable on all devices. Walk through and remove unused elements. Rename confusing items to simple names.
- Security Review — Act as a security reviewer and review: If this app safe for production use? How should I handle sensitive data (like API keys, user info)? Do my error messages expose any risks? What simple best practices should I add right now? Explain in plain English.
- Role Play Review — Act as a [choose role: Senior Software Developer, UI/UX Expert, QA Analyst, Product Owner, or Technical Consultant]. Review my app and answer: What do you notice first? What confuses you or feels unnecessary? What risks or weaknesses do you see? What’s the single most impactful improvement I should make next?
What Actually Worked
- Interface-first thinking: Designing the layout before adding functionality made every subsequent decision smaller and clearer.
- Structured prompting: The same prompt templates worked for recurring tasks — writing interface copy, choosing visual elements, testing flows.
- Disciplined scope: I avoided feature creep by asking one precise question per prompt and testing immediately after changes.
- Clean inputs: Well-organized source files mattered more than prompt optimization. Fifteen minutes cleaning PDFs saved hours of debugging.
- Role Play as review: Asking the AI to act as a QA tester, UX expert, and product owner revealed issues I wouldn’t have spotted as a builder.
The fewer choices I made, the more progress I made.
Critical Avoidances
- No tool switching mid-sprint: Stayed in one platform to maintain context
- No premature optimization: Built the happy path first, handled edge cases second
- No research rabbit holes: Quick prototyping answered questions faster than extensive planning
- No complex animations: Subtle fades only, after core functionality worked
- No skipping security checks: I asked the AI to review whether the app was “production ready.” It flagged safe storage of API keys and reminded me to avoid error messages that leak system details.
The Real Test
A colleague tried the app without instruction. She uploaded three research papers, asked about methodology differences, and saved two key insights to her notebook. No questions, no confusion.
That’s the validation that matters — not the code quality or feature completeness, but whether someone else can derive value without explanation.
Your Next 48-Hour Sprint
Pick one problem you face repeatedly. Before you build anything:
- Validate: Ask three people how they currently solve this problem
- Simplify: What’s the smallest version that provides real value?
- Design first: Sketch the interface before requesting code
- Test constantly: Use it yourself after every major change
- Ship early: Share with others while it still feels incomplete
The magic isn’t scale or sophistication — it’s building something that works reliably for its intended purpose.
Ready to try your own sprint? Start with validation, not code.
